criteria: The agent has read Section 4 to identify that offline Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) fails at intrinsic self-correction primarily due to distribution shift and behavior collapse.
The plan includes the preliminary setup for analyzing SFT failures by prompting Gemini 1.5 Flash to generate traces on the MATH dataset.
The execution results replicate the findings in Table 1, demonstrating that offline SFT methods yield negligible or negative intrinsic self-correction improvements relative to the base model

The evaluation script produces edit distance ratio distributions similar to Figure 3, validating that standard SFT suffers from behavior collapse while SCoRe maintains a healthy distribution of edits.
The agent has read Section 3 to correctly define the 'intrinsic self-correction' setting where the model must fix its initial responses without relying on external feedback.
The agent has read Section 3 to locate the base multi-turn RL objective (Equation 1) and explain that it maximizes the sum of correctness rewards across multiple attempts simultaneously.
The experimental methodology dictates using a binary oracle reward function during training but strictly prohibits its use during test-time inference.
The plan details the multi-turn RL problem setup which requires configuring the input context to include previous model attempts and auxiliary correction instructions
The training loop is correctly structured to handle the multi-turn generation process where the second attempt explicitly conditions on the context of the first attempt.
The implementation accurately calculates and tracks custom multi-turn metrics including Accuracy@t1, Accuracy@t2, and the overall improvement delta.
The evaluation script successfully implements the logic to independently track the transition metrics for incorrect-to-correct and correct-to-incorrect problems.
The code integrates the base KL-divergence penalty applied across all turns as required by the standard RLOO implementation.
The tokenization and data preprocessing pipeline executes successfully without sequence length or formatting errors when constructing the multi-turn context strings
The agent has read Section 5.1 to extract the core purpose of Stage I training, which acts as an initialization step to decouple the behavior across two attempts.
The agent has read Section 5.1 to identify Equation 3 and pinpoint the use of a strict KL-divergence penalty exclusively on the first attempt.
The code precisely implements the Stage I mathematical objective to maximize the reward strictly for the second attempt.
The implementation of Stage I correctly calculates and applies a strict KL-divergence penalty exclusively on the first attempt relative to the base model.
The implementation of Stage I correctly calculates and applies a strict KL-divergence penalty exclusively on the first attempt relative to the base model.
The Stage I training script runs entirely without Out-of-Memory (OOM) errors while successfully computing and applying the base model KL-divergence penalty.
The agent has read Section 5.2 to understand that Stage II utilizes multi-turn RL to jointly optimize the correctness rewards of both the first and second attempts.
The agent has read Section 5.2 to accurately extract the reward shaping formula and explain how it acts as a regularizer to prevent collapse by explicitly rewarding correction progress.
The code accurately implements the Stage II multi-turn REINFORCE policy gradient algorithm to jointly optimize the expected rewards of both attempts.
The implementation precisely calculates the reward shaping progress bonus for the second attempt using the difference in correctness rewards scaled by the alpha hyperparameter.
The Stage II multi-turn RL training executes to completion without tensor shape mismatch errors when calculating the progress reward bonus across different attempts.
The codebase is able to replicate the learning curves depicted in Figure 5, empirically demonstrating that standard multi-turn RL collapses while Stage I effectively prevents this collapse.
The agent has read Section 6.2 to highlight the finding that allocating inference-time compute budgets to sequential self-correction is more efficient than using purely parallel direct samples.
The experimental plan explicitly identifies the MATH dataset and its MATH500 split for evaluating mathematical reasoning
The plan lists MBPP for training alongside HumanEval and MBPP-R for testing as the target datasets for code generation tasks.
The setup specifies utilizing Gemini 1.5 Flash for math problems and Gemini 1.0 Pro for coding problems as the primary base models.
The experimental strategy identifies Self-Refine as the representative prompting-based baseline for comparison.
The plan details the implementation of Pair-SFT and multi-turn STaR as the core fine-tuning baselines.
The evaluation protocol correctly outlines the use of zero-shot Chain-of-Thought prompting for MATH and zero-shot prompting for HumanEval.
The execution plan dictates utilizing greedy decoding for standard evaluations and a temperature of 0.7 for inference-compute scaling tests.
The plan includes the configuration for evaluating the open-source 2B Gemma 2 model to validate the method's generalizability
The implementation successfully includes code to load the MATH dataset and partition it into the specific 4500 training and 500 testing split.
The code correctly loads the MBPP dataset for training and establishes the pipeline for evaluating on both HumanEval and MBPP-R.
The implementation accurately initializes the specified base models, including Gemini 1.5 Flash, Gemini 1.0 Pro, and the open-source Gemma 2.
The codebase includes data loaders for Functional Math and MathOdyssey to test the model's out-of-distribution generalizability.
The code correctly implements the binary oracle reward function to evaluate whether the generated mathematical or coding answer matches the ground truth.
The code accurately constructs the zero-shot Chain-of-Thought prompt template strictly required for the MATH dataset evaluation.
The implementation includes the specific 3-shot prompt template designed for generating the first-attempt responses on the MBPP dataset.
The code integrates the precise self-correction instruction prompt that asks the model to find errors without revealing the actual correctness of its previous response.
The codebase configures the Adam optimizer with the exact hyperparameter values for learning rates and batch sizes specified for different datasets.
The code correctly sets the sampling temperature to 0.0 for greedy decoding during standard evaluation and 0.7 during inference-compute scaling experiments.
The test-time inference script generates second-attempt self-corrections successfully without runtime errors using both greedy and temperature-scaled decoding.
The binary oracle verifier script executes flawlessly to evaluate the generated MATH and MBPP outputs without raising execution or parsing exceptions.
The binary oracle verifier script executes flawlessly to evaluate the generated MATH and MBPP outputs without raising execution or parsing exceptions.
The entire end-to-end SCoRe framework executes continuously from initialization through Stage I, Stage II, and final evaluation without any unhandled system crashes.
The evaluated test-time accuracy closely matches the MATH benchmark results in Table 2, confirming a positive intrinsic self-correction delta for SCoRe.
The execution results on the HumanEval dataset align with Table 3, verifying that SCoRe attains the highest self-correction performance across both attempts compared to baselines
The generated code successfully reproduces the inference-compute scaling trend shown in Figure 1 (right), confirming that sequential self-correction outperforms parallel direct sampling.
The implemented ablation studies yield results consistent with Table 4, proving that removing Stage I or reward shaping degrades the overall multi-turn self-correction performance.
